What you want is a headline that works, that's been tested, that's been proven. And a lot of people think that they have to do something different when they really need to do something that works or gets person's attention or sells. But in the open quotes, uh, the no closing quotes, they found that you know, you know, a person like Christine in the past might have had a connection that if you open the quote on the headline and don't close it, it keeps people reading and I've seen it where it happens on like a double headline or, uh, um, or like a lead in or something where they use the open quote, they won't close it and they'll open quote the next paragraph of the next sentence. And so they found that by doing that, it is increased readership. So that's a little secret, um, that may help you test it out. I just, the other thing that I want to talk about is, is this is probably, well, if I read all the headlines, this would be great for certain things. But, um, one of the things that my dad did and, um, people like Scott Haynes do, um, uh, and even I think that clock, enough clock Hawkins, um, Thomas Hall did was they kept all these index cards of headlines and they went through them over and over and over again looking for ideas, sparkers. And there's a guy, we might be, I might be able to pull a favor. There was a guy that had created an app where you put in certain basic grammars about your product you want to sell and it instantly create all these classic headlines with your blanks filled in for you. So you could like go through them and look at them and say, Hey, that one looks good for you. That one really sells well. Um, but the thing is, is that when I went through all these headlines and I was doing creating the slideshow for everyone, um, even if I was blocked, my brain was being having all, you know, almost forced me to come up with headlines. It forced me to think about headlines. It got my ideas and juices going on headlines, even if I didn't write one of the headlines that was in there, it really zeros you in on thinking about the headline, focusing on the headline and, and getting that energy going where you don't have to try to force yourself. If you just look at those headlines and read them, they will, um, uh, they will get you going. So go ahead, Chrissy. I figured if I gave you a visual, you'd know, we do have a couple of questions here. Mary is apparently on fire. She's got stuff she wants to know. Um, the first one is that, uh, strong verbs definitely seem important. That's a good tip. Are there any words you'd advise us against using? And is there a limit for how long you'd drag a headline on? Yes and no, um, there are headlines that have been, um, one word and there have been headlines that have literally been like a page long headlines. It really is going to depend on what you're trying to say in the message. You're getting, I think last week I talked about the idea that you ran out, uh, that I think, you know, back in the old print media days, but, um, and I know this has been done before where you printed a very large headline. And then as they got in the body copy to make space for the ad, the font size of the text got smaller and smaller and smaller because they were more invested. They were more interested. They were more sucked in though. They'll hold the thing two inches from the face. If it's interesting enough to keep reading. Um, so, uh, I don't know that a headline could be too long. It must grab them. It must invest them. And if you think about what the body copy is, the, the, the, every, all the great pros talk about the headline being just the next sentence of the headline. The body copy is just the next sentence of the headline. Okay. Um, there's a lot of people who would like to go a headline and then give you the history of something in, in like slowly heading, like, you see that in, in, uh, uh, editorials, you see that they'll give you this headline and then they'll rewind to this thing where they go back and blah, blah, blah. You know, just got off the airplane and he'd been traveling for three days and blah, you know, you know, there's, you don't want to do that. You want that headline to drop straight continuation into what you, what the thought you put in their head and you want to continue it and, and deep in, deep in their interest in it and deep in that historian emotion. So is there a too long? Um, I mean, the only, I would say that a single sentence, uh, shouldn't be too long. That'll drive people crazy. That's what lawyers do to make, uh, contracts complicated to read is to make a sentence that's, uh, incredibly long and very hard to read. And you have to keep, keep several concepts in your head when you really just want to inject one into their head. So I don't know if that answered the question. The other thing is some words you want to stay away from just one second. One word, some words you want to stay from are two words you want to stay away from, particularly in, um, certain, um, um, certain markets are miracle and sure those are two words you absolutely want to tiptoe around when you're talking about certain things. Um, but, uh, there are, there's been a lot of controversy over certain headlines being too salacious of stuff, which I don't believe. I think there's, um, too, um, profane for certain markets. Um, but, um, as far as no, no words, um, just, just, I think anything that, that is not emphatic or absolute or passionate, um, anything that's tepid, I would stay away from, I'm sorry, but I'm going to go ahead. No, I just wanted to get a couple of things in on the answer. Um, I agree that the headline really can't be too long. You know, I mean, but that's when you talk about chopping out every word, you'll chop a rat out every word that doesn't earn a space up in the headline. But don't forget that there's a lead in headline and you can do what is like a two headlines where you have a main headline, which is the big bold one. So you can take that really long one and like have the middle one, the, the beginning be sort of the lead in, then you have the main headline and it kind of continues in the slightly lower font, but it's still a bold headline thing. So when you were going through all these, um, all these power words, I started writing them down and forming a headline here. It turned out to be really long and they did that with it. And I wrote attention all copywriters new website reveals how to get your free report with bond, how it's amazing, secret editing formula. This amazing system will work even if, um, even for a desperate new wordsmith and make them sound like a seasoned pro, even if they suck. But the point is, and I hit every word, except the, the one, I was shocked that you didn't say desperate yet. Um, I thought about it. I actually did cross my mind. Oh, I know. I believe you did insist. But the loads of desperate murder, those are those passion words. Yes. Yeah. And again, I think that you should go with the ones that you feel are comfortable. But no, there's no words that would be off limits. And I do want to make it clear that when Kevin says you stay with miracle and cure, they're legal words that, um, that's why he's recommending staying away because if you use those words, it's a big no, no with the FTC. But that's not true with certain things. You know, I mean, if you're talking about something that's obviously not going to be a miracle cure for cancer or an illness, you know, when you say this new system, you know, teaching my kids how to tie their shoes and it was like a miracle, that's, that's okay. Um, but yeah. So I mean, I'm just reemphasizing that. And I just want to point out, you can use the lead in to visually make the headline look less, you know, um, less lengthy by using a lead in and a smaller, it's kind of like, I don't know what we never found out the words like a subhead except right below the headline. And it's not, it's not inside. Right. We didn't have that last week. Yeah. Anyway. Yeah. Did you talk about that last week? I'm sorry. I just wanted to add to that, which is, um, in the headlines, one of the other things, um, just if you're, if someone watches this happens to be in that category that we're talking about, um, the FDA and the FTC are people you want to be careful with on that stuff. But the other thing, if you're in that market, you want to be careful about is, um, uh, if you name a disease. So you can say, do your joints say, but do you say, do you have arthritis? And if you're offering a solution and you use the word arthritis, then you get into a whole other category. Um, it's just amazing that if you, if you go, do you have arthritis, you know, and you go blah, blah, blah, and your product is a book, they will literally write you a letter and saying that, uh, that classifies your book as a drug. Uh, Mary wants to know what are solutions and I don't know, um, how much you and bond have dealt with this in email. I know that Mark has for sure. And I have, um, a lot of the buzzwords don't make it past spam filters. And she wants to know what are the tips for dealing with that? A couple of things is, is that free, we used to be a big no, no, and actually free can get through. Um, it turns out how you use it. There's all kinds of algorithms that say you can and cannot use certain things. A lot of times they get into, um, phrases. So they get into like really complex, you can do this and you can do that. Um, and it kind of depends how much they think you're a spammer to begin with. But if you have like, you know, a hundred percent open rate and you got a hundred percent people responding to you and, uh, you're saying a bunch of stuff, really, they're going to keep you out because there's an indication that there's a relationship, um, to get around those things. One of the words that, uh, I see J. Abraham use is gratis or my compliments. Um, you know, or no cost to you. Um, it depends on what word you're trying to get passed, um, to get past them as to, um, you know, if you're being blocked for an Nigerian scam or something like that. Um, so if you have particular words, I mean, we can definitely, um, consider working here together, Mary, and telling you, uh, what you're trying to get past a spam filter. Um, one of the things that I'll just tell you that helps get past spam is images. Um, for some reason, spammers don't use images. I think it might be after, because they're going after the hosting of the image and they can block the hosting of the image. So anyone who's legit, if you put an image in you just, you just increase your deliverability by having an image in there. And you just create, click through eight and your open rate and your attention. And go ahead. Yeah, I was going to say, and no one word will get you banned from the spam. Uh, you know, when you, I've been on a lot of stuff through spam assassin and, you know, it's the totality of it that piles up. And I find it very easy to just, you know, avoid, you know, um, you know, certain words, like, you know, for example, they might not like make money where you're, when you say make living or make the kind of living, you know, the, um, but the, the point is it's really not a big concern anymore as much as it used to be because everybody's really focused more on engagement. Anyway, so don't, you know, the only time that you really want to go in there is when your spam assassin rating is in the yellow zone, that zone that lets you know it's right near the edge, but do not worry and try and get it down to a zero rating. And that, that'll be detrimental. It's not really the point. That's why it's done in the totality. So I've used the word free several times. Go ahead and use it if that's what you're actually using. And you remember it's getting past spam filters is kind of like the secrets to SEO. Become who they're looking for. And as long as you really aren't spamming people, it's going to help, you know, that every time they pass these kind of weird rules and regulations, it helps people who aren't spamming. So, um, I don't, I really don't worry that many about the words. If, if, if I had that over the limit with the words, I would just break out of this ours and be no problem. All right. You know what I would do is get them out of the email, you know, get them out of the email as fast as possible. The other thing, if I was being sneaky, I'd run into my animated gifts that put words on the screen and they couldn't read it because it's a picture. You know what I mean? I could put free all day long in an animated gift until they OCR that image. They ain't going to know what's going on there. Um, um, or run a video or an audio track. Um, which I created a product a long time ago about how to, how to, uh, create animated gifts, which everybody kind of love. Um, one other suggestion that I would make for somebody who's, you know, maybe new, maybe doesn't have given facility for animation, uh, is if you put a text box in rather than just typing it directly in your doc. Um, if you do a text box for part of your, uh, text, or if you create a JPEG image of the text, like you just type it in your Word document and then throw it over into like Microsoft publisher and created JPEG of that piece of text, the spam filter doesn't read it as text. It never tried it. Yeah. I would want to be caught, but if, if I were brave enough, yes, but if you know what works, I'd never tried it. I mean, I would just try to always stay on the good side because I'm trying to mail more than one email. Right. But does Mark have some solutions to that? I don't know. Mark, do you have anything to add to that? Um, with the spam filters, I think there's a movement away now from, uh, and it depends who you're with. If you're sending a few hundred emails, um, uh, to a newsletter, then it's irrelevant. You can use any word that you like. If you're sending, uh, hundreds of thousands or millions of emails a day, then it becomes a different kettle of fish. And what you've got to realize is that the ISPs aren't looking so much at the, um, the words anymore. They're looking a lot more about, um, you know, how many people you send to and how many people are opening, for example, you know, so how many people, if you knock on enough doors, how many people are opening those doors. So that's an indication to the spam filters that it's not, um, it could be spam email if you've got a high percentage of people who are not opening. It could also be that, um, uh, you know, the, how old you're, the domain is, the sending those emails. There's a lot of other criteria that I think are more important than particular words and emails now, and we've eased off a lot. We use a service called E-FoTrait, I think it's called, where we used to every email that we write, we will pass through that. Um, and that will tell us, it will highlight the words that are spam and simultaneously give us alternative words to, uh, those words. So free is a big one, right? So free used to be a big one and the obvious one was gratis or complimentary or, um, no costs or won't cost your penny. And there's an upside and a downside that if you write a long email and you pass it through a spam filter, you're going to have five, 10, 15 Watts that don't pass a spam filter. And if you change them all, they can change the tone of the email. So there's a balance there, but a lot of us got to do with the size of, uh, how many people you're mailing. So, you know, if you're mailing thousands or hundreds of thousands of people, you've got to be more aware. And, um, and what happens to them, a lot of people, uh, and our emails, for example, the biggest, um, group of people use Gmail. So in Gmail, you have different boxes. You have a box, your inbox, and then you have a box for promotions, uh, and a box for, uh, offers. I was going to jump over to it quickly. So you get three different types of boxes. You get primary promotions and updates that are set by default. So the way that an email is written, um, can be, uh, will they be determined which box that goes into? So we have quite a success of getting the box into the primary inbox because we try and like, write a lot of our emails like an informal email. And it changes. It's a moving target. So, you know, it used to be if you had a date affected, you know, your deliverability, if you had today's date in it, or if you had newsletter, or if you had something that certain keywords would trigger things. Um, uh, images as well. There, you know, it used to be an old trick. People would put most of their high peak content in the image and have texts around it. Um, that was very popular with casinos. Um, but most of all of the tricks and things like that, that people used to do have fallen away. Uh, and that's a lot of what it's got to do with relationship. So, um, how, you know, if you've got a good relationship with the people that you're talking to, there'll be enough interaction, people opening it or moving it to certain boxes or doing something with that that tells the ISP that it's okay. So in summary, I wouldn't get hung up anymore on spam. You're, if you use an auto responder service, they will tell you if it's over the top and then you can look at it, but just write the way that you want to write. Okay. Um, Mark, did you have anything else that you wanted to add with regard to what you were talking about, um, on the copies? No, I think we'll cover most of that. I think I'm going to hand things back over to Kevin then. Oh, no, I think I did. I think I covered most of it. Finish your, you finished your power. Well, we've got a few lines forever. Um, I'm just trying to think, you know, there's a lot of things that we could, um, try to think. Uh, let me think. Let me just think if there's any broad strokes. Um, he, um, but here's a couple of the things that we've picked up in testing. If you put it in inverted commas, that would generally lift response can lift it as much as 10% just by having your, your headline and inverted commas. No full stop. Uh, if it's an inverted commas, we've found that, uh, having a color inside a headline, if it's black and you're throwing some red there, it can lift response. Um, the part that the sub headline under the headline can be read as much. As the headline itself. And it's more important. The page generally converts better if you pay off the headline, which can, uh, which Kevin has mentioned before in a conversation with Garib Bence of Enga, whatever your headline is, it should have something to do with what you're writing about. So if you can sort of lead into the story from your headline, um, that can make for a very compelling read, um, the use through the page of sort of little baby headlines, um, cause people don't read, they scan. So, and one of the reasons we found is that, uh, the, you know, it makes up, say somewhere between 30 and 50% of the results of your sales page are going to be determined by your headline because it's what determines how long people read the rest of your copy. So if you think of how long a few seconds is, you know, if you just count one, two, three, you've read most headlines in the world. If it's not good, you don't get a reader to carry on reading. So that's, you know, the reason why the headline is so important is it draws the reader in. That's the first thing they see. So, uh, the whole psychology of psychology of the page can be the headline. Um, I read something about Picasso once he said, um, paraphrasing here. I said the whole painting is determined by the first stroke. In other words, however it turns out is determined by that first stroke. And when you write as a copywriter, usually your first sentence can determine everything that comes after it. Um, and there's a lot of theories about the writing headline before or after or during, but for the reader, the first part is always a headline, which is why it's the most important. Yeah. I'd also like to say that, um, when you talk about paying off the headline in emails specifically, cause you know, we're also talking about subject line creation here, the, um, the engagement will be better if they see that the beginning of the email looks like it's about to follow through on what the promise is. So you don't actually give it away right away in that first line. What you do is kind of reiterate. And actually one of the things you can do is literally just copy the subject line and make that look like the title of the report that they're about to get, that they're about to open up. And, um, that will also, it improves your engagement. The other thing I'd like to add is that, um, Kevin brought it up and did a pretty good job on it. I was going to talk to him about curiosity more, but one of the ways that he kind of mentioned and touched on it was numbers. Do your best. If there's always a way to slip in a number, it doesn't matter if it's a date, you know, amazing money-making secrets of 2016. That's more specific than of 2015. It can be a wait. It can be a number of steps. It can, you know, there's almost always a number that you can add in there. It's not an absolute. There are a lot of great headlines without them, but that number, all the adding any kind of number always adds to that curiosity and the specific that there's going to be a specific benefit or payoff to opening it. And that, that does help a lot. So don't, when it comes to numbers, you know, be creative about it. You're going to find them. And lastly, like we said yesterday, create yourself a, create yourself a swipe file of headlines, stuff that's kind of attractive to you, that's got kind of power and some emotion to it that kind of grabs you, that you can peruse through to not only get inspiration, to get inspiration and ideas, but write several. You know, I mean, I think the one thing that we'll all agree on is, you know, the best copy and stuff like that. People have gone, you know, there are alternative headlines for so many of these successful promotions. And, you know, that, you want, you know, that's something that you're also, it's probably your most valuable testing is testing different headlines. It's probably the most valuable kind of change or variable you can taste, or excuse me, test. That's all I wanted to add. That's good. Do we have any questions or anything else? Oh, for storytelling in copy. How do you best recommend tying in the headline to make sure it flows? The the the life, my favorite, as far as the storytelling there's several, I know my mic's really hot. The the the thing is, is that what you really want to do with the storytelling part is that the students to storytelling ones that come to my mind are the amazing diet secret of a desperate housewife from Ohio and the amazing direct male secret of a nerd from Ohio. And what you what I I like those two because they cover some of the secrets of storytelling. One is, is that when you say the amazing diet secret of a desperate housewife, you don't think this went badly. You think there was a solution. There was a great ending. Okay. So the what was it? There was one for Jay Abraham to it was a it was amazing. I think it was amazing. Marketing secret of a marketing genius that's afraid genius. Yeah. Amazing secret of the marketing genius who's afraid to fly. Yeah. And look at all those words that we used in there that have been used everywhere. The thing was is that when the openers of those things were a couple things. The first thing was is it in the storytelling, it became about a person. It became it became a conversation, a media you conversation. It was not a broadcast talking to the world kind of thing. It was a personal storytelling. And the first thing that was being done in like, like I'm thinking of the desperate housewife diet is the first thing they're doing is we talked about this in detail is they are identifying themselves. First thing they said was, hi, my name is blah, blah, blah. I'm this and I'm that. I'm I live in Ohio. I, you know, a mother of three kids stuff this up. Right. That's the first thing. And the reason we talked about that was because that's the first thing that they're thinking is who are you and why should I listen to you? Or even more important things, they may not be thinking about it, but they're going to start thinking about it. And you might as well get it out of the way. And you might as well start drawing a picture and humanizing yourself in the reader's mind of the who you're talking to, because that's going to come up later anyways. And you don't want them sitting there going to the end looking at your PS and as a pitch, you want them to read your story before they know that this is a pitch or that, you know, it feels it's going to be harder. So when you get down there, because it's supposed to be, it's supposed to cook that frog slowly and get more into more pitching and more sales at the end. So that's the other thing. But the other thing you was, it was like, you know, it happened last June. I just, I felt like I was, I just broke down and started to cry. I, you know, I felt like I was going to have a nervous breakdown where it worked with some of the things. And the interesting part is, is that the first headline tells you there's going to be a solution or happy ending or something irrelevant that you're going to want to know how they get there. You remember we talked about that Hollywood kiss and we have to figure out why it's such a valuable thing or what they went through. But the interesting thing is the second sentence, it's like, we showed the end and now we're showing the beginning. And now people are invested in what happens between those two spots. And particularly when you call out the reader and make them invested in this story, being their story. Okay. Because they came in, they came in the door because they, you sold them on the other headline, you sold them, you have a solution to what they are suffering from. The second thing he was, is you rewound the clock to the beginning. And what was happening to this person, you set the scene as to what is going on. What is it? How did this, how did this start out? And you, and we talked about this before, you want to start out farther back to where they are in a worse position than the reader. So the reader has no excuse. Oh, I had these obstacles. I can't do it because of this. I can't do it. But you've rewound it to the point where the person in the story was, is happier than you now, but was worse off than them when you started, worse off than the reader when they started. And so that's when you come in. And that's when you start, when you get into that, the way that story goes is you're just telling the reader his story. Okay. You're telling the reader his story. And so all you're doing is dimensionalizing, dimensionalizing what's coming from the headline into, into a point where you are, are, are more and more investing the reader into the story because the story is their story. So that's, that's how Gary, how would do it? You come in and say, here's, here's a happy ending, big promise. What you care about most, dominant emotion in your headline. And here am I, this is who I am because this is how we start introductions. And then you start in that story and you start continuing right, right into that. And also your stories are always going to be the first person or third person. Sometimes for branding purposes, it looks better than somebody else is telling you about that third person. And the change is really simple. Instead of, hi, my name is so and so, which is the way it's always is. If it's a first person story, it's, his name is so and so. And you'll probably start with where he lives. You know, he's a local, you know, that makes him real kind of down to earth. There's a local marketer in Australia who has been doing these incredible, wonderful things and so forth. And then you start to tell, but, you know, it wasn't always that way or however it goes. But again, I'm going to reiterate what Kevin said, just in a shorter, shorter way, which is all stories have a change. It's, they went from one place, then there's the change, and then that's where they ended up. And you always start from, you know, back where they started. And back when they started, I just interject there is a, is a high emotion moment. Yeah. You know, they weren't humming along, walking home from school, blah, blah, blah.
